title: Reduce Swelling and Bruising When Injecting. Dr Tim’s Top Tips To Reduce Trauma
filename: Reduce Swelling and Bruising When Injecting. Dr Tim’s Top Tips To Reduce Trauma.en.vtt
excerpt: On this week's show I reflect with Charlotte on how I managed to reduce swelling over time without even trying it reveals some of the insights around what causes swelling and how your injection strategy can massively change the experience that patients have after having a procedu…

segments.text: on this week's show I reflect with Charlotte on how I managed to reduce swelling over time without even trying it reveals some of the insights around what causes swelling and how your injection strategy can massively change the experience that patients have after having a procedure Charlotte was really acutely aware that swelling was a big issue for many patients and wants to reduce it and in this show I discuss all the ways that I've done that in my practice in terms of avoiding other sort of side effects is there any sort of way that you can reduce swelling on patients I'm worried that I'm going to do someone so I've got someone booked for lips in a few weeks my first time and I'm really worried about her going out with absolutely massive lips so how can I reduce that risk what you're really worried about is an upset patient yeah and so preparation is key I would tell them she's going to swell and then it's useful to know some sort of dynamics of what that swelling is like so peaks in about six hours and then it starts to fade a little bit sometimes a bit worse than the morning after as well and then it starts to improve 80 to 90 of people within five days is returned to normal to look at but they can still feel it's a little

bit swollen in terms of reducing swelling there are lots of things the first thing is don't do unnecessary injections like you don't have to do you know 40 vertical injections on every patient if someone's particularly worried about swelling vary your Technique so you may have learned this on our day you can do a technique where you enter through one entry point and do three injections essentially and that means you can do most of the top lip with four injections and that that'll have less swelling than someone who's gone in multiple times every time you stick the needle in and blood that causes swelling and going through tearing through cells are the more injections you do the more swelling you get yeah so try and reduce your injections the next thing is a big one for me because I remember getting loads of bruises when I first started and then they stopped and I remember thinking well my patients don't bruise anymore it's like Well it can't be my patience it was because of precision the one of the earliest skills you need to develop is the resolution for your Anatomy so a new injector can't tell when it's two millimeters deeper than an experienced injector and so you're going in through tissue that's more vascular and you're

causing more trauma but it looks even on video it can look pretty much the same but you're getting different results so in that moment when you're injecting really think about where you're trying to be and I kind of Zone in and just my whole world becomes that little needle space and I'm really thinking about gliding in exactly in a plane I want to be and that seems to have an impact on the number of bruises that you get it's a bit like taking blood isn't it you sort of imagining where your needle is it sat on the top of the vessel is it on the bottom why am I feel like I'm in the vessel but nothing's coming so I suppose it's like that but just trying to avoid it the other way isn't it exactly that kind of thinking so if you want to be in the lip you want to be just under the mucosa you don't want to be in the middle of the lip for if you're depending on where you're trying to inject but you know all of those blood vessels are within kind of three to five millimeters and the big hematomas you get when you go you've gone through an archery most of them have balloon up straight away and that's usually from being too deep not always but typically it's a it's a deep injection and if you ever see like someone showed me recently of a huge hematoma and a vo and

they had the hematoma one they had it on camera and I saw this injected it was just the most barbaric injection it's like well no wonder that's why I don't get hematomas because I don't Ram the needle up your nostril through your lip yeah so um if you're thinking this way you're already going to be on that side of the spectrum slow gentle and another thing is just stability you know make sure you're stabilized so from your wrist only thing moving should be your fingers and slow and steady all the way through and you'll have less swelling you can also treat the swellings right after so that once they've had the procedure you could preemptively give them some ibuprofen some people think it improve increases bruising yeah because it thins the blood yeah I don't know because I know they do surgery on people's ibuprofen I know why ibuprofen has been wrapped into it um aspirin absolutely makes bruising worse I will say more the difference between patients is much bigger than the difference between a patient who's had aspirin and a patient who hasn't like you can have a someone who's not taking anything it's a massive bruise and someone who's on Clopidogrel and all this stuff and doesn't seem to bruise some people are just bruises aren't they

I'm a bit worried as well with like after doing fillers about massaging the area that I'm going to move the filler is that can you move it and manipulate it and then I'm gonna put it above the lip and then look overfilled and migrated I think it's an excellent thought and you should think about that so the way I would suggest you do is you think of it not so much of it as a massage as a sculpting movement so imagine the lip is made out of plasticine and you're shaping it so I would I feel along the inside of the lip very gently in a way that wouldn't move product to feel if I can feel any irregular lumps or bumps and if I find one I gradually increase the pressure on that slowly in a way that if I the way I'm increasing the pressure would put the filler where I want it to be so I'm typically got adding the Vermilion border and just gently smoothing it rather than just mashing in and crushing it so it's um I sometimes you imagine if you're in a you know if you're blind and you're in a room or the lights off and there's a pee on a plate and you just want to squash that P and not make a mess of anything else you'd kind of feel

for it first yeah Zone in on it until your finger was just over the top of it and then you gently push it until it popped right yeah it's more that than just massaging everywhere yeah I think like Julie Holland says like she scratches it out don't you like have his little lump so just it's a gentle sculpt not a getting in their massage because then that can increase swelling as well can't it yeah it will do and so that's particularly if you're doing certain injections if you end up basically in The Superficial part of the lip then you end up with a superficial lump and that needs to be massaged in a different way than most of the lumps you get which are little bonuses that you can feel yeah and be aware that sometimes what you're feeling is a hematoma particularly if it's later on like they come back the next day with a lump yeah if it's tender Then I then I usually would just leave it you've got some swelling and inflammation it's not the filler there's non-tender that's the filler and you can massage it away and if someone's come in for lips that I want to build up slowly so doing maybe not Point sevens in one session how long would you leave it then until the next session if they're wanting to build up slowly no you want to wait until the swelling is not a big factor because

you're gonna have so much swelling that you can't see what you're doing even in the first week you don't know how much about there what you're seeing is just inflammation rather than the filler so two weeks is typically what people leave at least yep okay lovely thank you foreign [Music] foreign
